does: This paper introduces VL-SAE, a sparse autoencoder designed to interpret vision-language model representations by mapping them into a unified concept space. The core contribution is developing a method where each neuron in the autoencoder's hidden layer corresponds to a human-interpretable concept, demonstrated through semantically similar images and text that activate the same neurons consistently. The approach uses explicit alignment based on cosine similarity to measure multi-modal semantic similarity, then trains the sparse autoencoder with a distance-based encoder and modality-specific decoders to enforce activation consistency across vision and language inputs, thereby creating an interpretable bottleneck for understanding how VLMs align different modalities.
connects: This work builds on recent efforts to interpret deep neural networks through sparse autoencoders and mechanistic interpretability, extending these techniques to the multi-modal setting. It responds to growing concerns about the opacity of vision-language alignment—a central component enabling strong performance in models like CLIP and other contrastive VLMs. The paper combines ideas from representation alignment research, which seeks to understand how different modalities map to shared semantic spaces, with sparse feature discovery methods that have proven effective for interpreting single-modality models. It directly addresses a gap in the literature by targeting the under-investigated interpretability of the alignment mechanism itself rather than individual modality representations.
enables: By creating an interpretable concept set for vision-language alignment, this work opens avenues for diagnosing and improving VLM behavior. The unified concept representation could enable researchers to identify which semantic dimensions are well-aligned versus misaligned across modalities, potentially guiding targeted improvements to alignment quality. Future work could leverage these interpretable concepts for knowledge injection, bias detection and mitigation, and more controlled model editing. The method could also facilitate better evaluation of multi-modal understanding by testing whether models actually learn coherent cross-modal concepts rather than spurious correlations, and may inform the design of more interpretable next-generation vision-language architectures.
